strands.title: Replay Module Architecture & Requirements
strands.content: A new offline simulation module is being designed at src/pichay/replay.py to measure the effectiveness of context paging by replaying historical interactions. The module must parse proxy JSONL logs (produced by proxy.py), reconstruct message arrays as they existed at each API call, apply pager.py compaction logic to snapshots, detect page faults (cases where evicted content was re-requested), and report both per-session and aggregate metrics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). The module will expose a CLI interface via argparse accepting log file paths, configurable thresholds (--age-threshold default 4, --min-size default 500), and --json for structured output. This is a measurement and validation tool that treats paging as an offline, reproducible simulation problem rather than runtime enforcement.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs and reconstruct message arrays at each API call turn
Paging effectiveness is measurable through offline replay — comparing actual next actions against simulated evictions reveals page faults
Key metrics are fault rate, bytes saved, and reduction percentage, reported per-session and in aggregate
CLI interface requires --age-threshold (default 4), --min-size (default 500), --json flag, and accepts multiple log file paths

strands.title: Dependency Analysis & Pre-Implementation Requirements
strands.content: Before writing replay.py, three existing modules must be thoroughly studied: pager.py (contains the compaction logic that will be applied to message snapshots), proxy.py (defines the JSONL log format and logging behavior), and eval.py (may contain evaluation patterns and utilities relevant to metrics computation). This pre-read is critical because the replay module must faithfully reproduce pager's behavior on historical data, respect proxy's log structure, and potentially follow eval's established patterns for result reporting. Understanding these modules first prevents implementation errors and ensures style consistency.

key_claims.text: pager.py contains the compaction logic that replay.py will invoke on reconstructed snapshots
proxy.py defines the JSONL log format that replay.py must parse
eval.py may provide evaluation patterns and metrics computation utilities
Coding style and patterns must match existing modules for consistency

strands.title: Core Simulation Logic: Snapshot Reconstruction & Fault Detection
strands.content: The replay simulation operates on a snapshot-by-snapshot basis. For each turn in a session, the module reconstructs the messages array as it existed before that API call, applies pager compaction logic with configured thresholds, and then checks whether the model's actual next action (extractable from the log) references any content that was evicted during compaction. A match between an evicted item and a subsequent request constitutes a page fault. This logic requires careful state tracking across turns within a session, proper understanding of what 'eviction' means in the pager's algorithm, and reliable extraction of the 'actual next action' from the log. The simulation is deterministic and reproducible.

key_claims.text: Snapshot reconstruction requires tracking message array state before each API call turn
A page fault occurs when the model requests content that was evicted by pager compaction in the prior turn
Pager compaction logic is applied within the simulation loop, not at logging time
Simulation is deterministic given log input and configuration parameters

strands.title: Metrics & Reporting
strands.content: Two levels of metrics are required. Per-session metrics include fault count, fault rate (faults / total turns), total bytes saved, and reduction percentage (bytes saved / original size). Aggregate metrics roll these up across all sessions in the input. The --json flag triggers machine-readable output (likely JSON format) rather than human-readable text, enabling downstream analysis. The module must track both the state before and after compaction to compute bytes saved and reduction percentage accurately.

key_claims.text: Per-session metrics: fault count, fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage
Aggregate metrics are computed across all sessions in the input
Bytes saved is computable as (original message array size - compacted size) per turn

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific implementation details of pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py (their actual function signatures, data structures, logging format details)
declared_losses.why: These modules have not been provided; the task requires studying them before implementation. Their internal structure is unknown at this stage.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Precise definition of 'actual next action' and how to extract it from the log
declared_losses.why: The task mentions checking if the model's next action re-requested evicted content, but doesn't specify how the log encodes actions or what constitutes an 'action' in the context of message content.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Definition of 'evicted content' — whether it's individual messages, message chunks, or semantic segments
declared_losses.why: The pager compaction logic will define what gets evicted, but that logic hasn't been examined yet. The granularity of eviction affects how fault detection works.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Error handling and edge case strategies for malformed logs, missing fields, or incomplete sessions
declared_losses.why: The task focuses on the happy path; robustness patterns will likely come from studying eval.py and proxy.py, not specified in the requirements.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific performance requirements or optimization considerations for handling very large log files
declared_losses.why: The task doesn't mention scale; the module design might differ depending on whether logs are small or very large.

open_questions: What is the exact structure of the JSONL log format produced by proxy.py? (field names, data types, nesting)
How does pager.py define and implement compaction? What is the signature of the key function to call?
How is the model's 'next action' encoded in or extractable from the proxy log?
What constitutes an 'evicted' item in the context of the pager? Messages? Message subranges?
How should page faults be detected — exact string/ID matching, fuzzy matching, or semantic matching?
Should the replay module reconstruct a full message array in memory, or stream through the log?
Are there edge cases (e.g., first turn, session boundaries, malformed logs) that require special handling?
Should the per-session report be printed immediately after each session, or accumulated and printed at the end?
Does eval.py provide a template for metrics aggregation and reporting?
What Python version and dependencies (if any) are available for this module?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "READ pager.py thoroughly: identify the main compaction function, its parameters, its return value, and what it considers 'evicted' (both the data and the mechanism).",
  "READ proxy.py thoroughly: identify the JSONL log schema, what fields exist per log line, how sessions are identified, and how API calls and their results are represented.",
  "READ eval.py thoroughly: identify how metrics are computed, aggregated, formatted, and reported; look for patterns in CLI handling and output formatting.",
  "After reading, formulate precise data flow: (1) log line → parsed record, (2) records → session grouping → message array reconstruction, (3) compaction application → eviction tracking, (4) next action lookup → fault detection, (5) metrics aggregation → reporting.",
  "Determine the specific data structure (e.g., dict, dataclass, or custom object) needed to represent a snapshot, an eviction event, and a fault.",
  "Design error handling: what happens with malformed lines, missing sessions, or incomplete turns?",
  "Plan the CLI: argparse structure, input validation, output formatting (text vs. JSON).",
  "Establish a coding style checklist from existing modules: naming conventions, docstring format, function organization, import patterns."
]
